text:
Done — `flatten.js` now flattens arbitrarily deep nesting instead of just one level.

The change makes the function recursive. It walks each element of the input array; when an element is itself an array, it calls `flatten` on that element and pushes each returned value, otherwise it pushes the element as-is. Because the recursion bottoms out only on non-array elements, nesting at any depth collapses into a single flat list — `[1, [2, [3, [4]]]]` resolves all the way down to `[1, 2, 3, 4]`. Empty nested arrays like `[[], [1]]` contribute nothing and simply yield `[1]`, since an empty array produces no pushes.

One thing to flag honestly: per the constraints of this task I based this briefing on reading the source only — I did not run the test suite, so I can't report a passing result from execution. The logic does cover the deep cases the test is described as pinning, but if you want a verified green run, say so and I'll execute the tests.
